summary: Gruber catalogs notable details from Apple's iPod Touch Features Guide PDF, comparing it against the iPhone. He highlights hardware differences like the relocated sleep/wake button and lack of volume buttons, software version numbering that implies a shared OS lineage, and feature gaps in both directions — the Touch lacks calendar editing and the headphone clicker, while gaining a debug console for MobileSafari and closed captioning support. The post serves as a close reading of a product manual to surface what Apple chose to include and omit.
key_points: The iPod Touch runs system version 1.1, implying the iPhone shipped with 1.0 of what would become iOS
The Touch lacks hardware volume buttons and the headphone cable clicker, making pocket use significantly less convenient than the iPhone
Several features like the double-tap-space-for-period shortcut, closed captioning, and a Safari debug console appear on the Touch but not yet on the iPhone
The inability to add calendar events on the Touch is a puzzling omission given that contact editing works fine
money_quotes: Presumably version 1.0 was the version of mobile OS X that shipped with the iPhones.
That clicker is my very favorite thing about the iPhone's music player; I think it'll be a pain to use an iPod Touch that's in your pocket.
You really can't add events in the Calendar app on the iPod Touch. Lame.
Double-tap the space bar while typing to enter a period followed by a space. That shortcut isn't available (yet?) on the iPhone.
Optional Debug Console for MobileSafari. The iPhone doesn't (yet?) have this.
key_insight: A careful reading of the iPod Touch manual reveals that Apple was deliberately differentiating the Touch from the iPhone through small hardware and software omissions, even as both devices clearly shared the same underlying OS.
